params.text:
Why staying active protects against falls

One of the most effective ways to prevent falls is often the most overlooked: keeping people moving. Muscle strength and good balance are what keep us steady, and both fade quickly when a person stops using them. When someone becomes less active — often because they are afraid of falling — their legs weaken and their balance worsens, making a fall more likely rather than less. Encouraging safe, regular activity helps break this cycle. This does not mean strenuous exercise. Everyday movement counts: standing up from a chair without using the arms where it is safe to do so, walking a little further, and gentle strength and balance exercises. NICE recommends strength and balance training for older people at risk of falling, as it is one of the few measures shown to make a real difference.

Falls services and your role

You are not expected to prescribe exercises yourself, but you have an important part to play, and specialist help is available.

  • Physiotherapists: assess movement and set safe, individual strength and balance programmes.
  • Falls services or clinics: many areas have a dedicated team, often reached through the GP, who carry out a full assessment for people who fall repeatedly.
  • Occupational therapists: advise on equipment and on adapting the home.

Your role is to notice when someone is at risk or has started to fall, to raise it so a referral can be made, and then to encourage and support the exercises and safe mobility that are recommended. Praise effort, build confidence, and never rush a person or do for them what they can safely do for themselves.

params.question: Mrs Okafor has become nervous about walking since a minor fall and now spends almost all day sitting down. How can you best help reduce her risk of falling?

answers.text: Gently encourage safe activity and support any strength and balance exercises recommended for her, raising a referral if she needs one.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Keeping her safely active maintains strength and balance, and involving physiotherapy or a falls service gives her a programme suited to her needs.

answers.text: Encourage her to stay seated as much as possible, since sitting still removes the risk of falling.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Inactivity weakens muscles and worsens balance, which increases the risk of falling and reduces her independence.

answers.text: Do everything for her so that she never needs to stand or walk at all.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Taking over robs her of movement she needs to stay strong and steady; the aim is to support safe activity, not remove it.

answers.text: Tell her the fall was her own fault so that she is more careful in future.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Blame damages confidence and trust; she needs encouragement and practical support, not criticism.
